text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for the nephrectomy procedure, using a balloon dilator to insufflate and create the retroperitoneal space. The balloon dilator is inserted through an incision in the mid axillary line, positioned just above the iliac crest.

The surgeon adjusts the port placement for the DaVinci SI robot to access the lower pole tumor in the retroperitoneum. A balloon dilator is used to expand the space, followed by the insertion of a camera port using a Hassan type cannula with a balloon inside. The first port is placed posteriorly beneath the twelfth rib using a zero-degree scope.

The surgeon identifies the erector spinae muscle and the twelfth rib, positioning an eight millimeter robotic posterior port in the angle. Using a laparoscopic instrument, the peritoneum is pushed anteriorly to create space for two additional robotic ports, enabling a forearm robotic approach. The setup includes ports for the number two and number three robotic arms, the camera, and the number one arm, facilitating kidney retraction.

The assistant positions a 12mm port near the anterior suprailiac spine under direct vision, ensuring the peritoneum is pushed off anteriorly. The surgical team prepares to dock the DaVinci SI robot to begin the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins by identifying the posterior body wall, psoas muscle, and quadratus lumborum muscle. Using the robotic arms, the surgeon pushes the gerota's fascia away from the scope to create more space in the small surgical field.

The surgeon pushes Girota's fascia away to create more space in the surgical field. The scope is rotated 90 degrees to orient the psoas muscle transversely across the screen, establishing it as the first landmark. An incision is made in Girota's fascia, which is the space where the kidney resides, with attention to the top edge for future reference.

The surgeon continues the incision in Girota's fascia parallel to the psoas muscle, revealing perinephric fat and the kidney. The fourth arm is used to retract the kidney slightly. The surgeon identifies the ureter and gonadal vessels while progressing towards the hilum within the lower retroperitoneum.

The surgeon uses the Xi robotic system to perform a retroperitoneal nephrectomy, revealing the renal artery within minutes. The fourth robotic arm is utilized for retraction, effectively exposing the artery by retracting the kidney.

The surgeon identifies the renal artery and notes the presence of additional arteries, including a lower pole vessel. The renal vein is visible, and the surgeon discusses the possibility of clamping the artery or addressing segmental arteries due to the large tumor size. The assistant is cautioned about suction to maintain space within the retroperitoneal cavity.

The surgeon exposes the vein, utilizing the anatomical model for guidance. The surgical field reveals detailed vascular anatomy, with the vein clearly visible. The assistant is instructed to manage suction carefully to maintain visibility and space within the surgical area.

The surgeon examines an artery branch, potentially a branch off the main artery, while noting the presence of another vessel nearby. The anatomy is carefully assessed, considering the possibility of extra vessels in the area.

The surgeon explores the lower pole of the kidney, searching for a lower pole vessel identified in imaging. The ureter is visible, and large veins are present in the surgical field. The assistant manages suction carefully due to limited space without an air seal system.

The surgeon identifies the cut edge of Girota's fascia and begins working within this layer to locate the tumor. The perinephric fat is visible hanging outside Girota's fascia, which the surgeon addresses before proceeding.

The surgeon demonstrates the cut edge of Girota's fascia, emphasizing the importance of staying inside this layer to avoid entering the peritoneum. The large tumor, approximately six centimeters, is visible, encompassing the lower posterior portion of the kidney. The surgeon discusses controlling a large venous branch at the tumor's base due to its central location.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to assist in the dissection, pushing down on the tumor to improve access. The surgical team works above the tumor, identifying normal kidney tissue as a landmark. The surgeon carefully navigates around sticky perinephric fat, ensuring not to get too close to the kidney capsule to preserve it for closure. The tumor is large, encompassing almost the entire kidney.

The surgeon navigates around the lower pole tumor, ensuring the ureter is identified early to avoid accidental damage. The tumor is visible, and the surgeon works around its base, noting the presence of vessels in the area.

The surgeon identifies a vessel previously seen in imaging and prepares to use endoscopic ultrasound to assess the tumor depth on the renal surface. The ultrasound will help plan the margin of resection and visualize deep tumor projections, enhancing surgical precision.

The surgeon identifies a large vessel within the perinephric fat, which appears to supply the tumor. The vessel is visible in the imaging and is located in the fat surrounding the kidney. The team discusses using the DaVinci robot's Firefly capability to enhance visualization during dissection, particularly for differentiating perfusion between the tumor and normal kidney tissue.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to visualize the tumor and mark the margin of resection. The ultrasound reveals a large tumor with projections into the sinus fat, which were previously noted on the CT scan. The surgeon adjusts the probe angle to differentiate between normal kidney tissue and the tumor, marking the tumor edge for resection.

The surgeon begins working on the upper portion of the kidney, focusing on the tumor base using high-quality ultrasound imaging. Numerous vessels are visible at the base of the tumor, which protrudes deeply into the sinus. The surgeon plans a nucleation approach, taking a small margin of cortical tissue before reaching the tumor, ensuring not to start the incision directly on the tumor surface.

The surgeon exchanges instruments, switching the retracting arm to the arm closest to the camera for better exposure. The ultrasound probe is removed, and the surgeon adjusts the position of the Prograsp instrument more anteriorly to optimize movement and assistance. The imaging suggests the presence of another vessel near the lower pole artery, which the surgeon plans to address.

The surgeon identifies a large artery and a giant vein in the surgical field, deciding to take the vessel out of the way. The imaging reveals a significant vessel that could not be ignored due to its size, potentially complicating the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a lumbar vein, noting its position as it extends downward. The gonadal vein is also mentioned, believed to be located higher up in the surgical field. Smoke is being suctioned from the area to maintain visibility.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and traces the gonadal vein, revealing a large, unexpected vein not visible on imaging. The surgeon examines the vein's origin and notes its unusual size, suggesting it may be a branch or connected to an artery.

The surgeon examines the anatomy, identifying the aorta and a segmental artery that appears to be curling away from it. The dissection continues with careful exploration of the vessel's origin and path, using suction to clear the field.

The surgeon traces a vessel that appears to curl in front of the main renal vein, identifying it as a segmental artery originating from the main renal artery. The ureter and kidney are visible in the surgical field. The surgeon plans to clamp the vessels using three bulldogs and a bolster, preparing to cut and bag the specimen with needle drivers, followed by a four-oh stitch.

The surgeon instructs the assistant to push into the surgical field, preparing for a segmental excision by cutting into normal tissue. The pneumo is adjusted to facilitate the procedure, focusing on excising without involving the vein.

The surgeon clips vessels while working at the edge of the surgical site, revealing the fat of the renal hilum. A metal five-millimeter instrument is used to clip a large vein, which will be sutured later.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection through the renal cortex into the sinus, revealing the collecting system and tumor. A large vessel is clipped multiple times for secure access, with suction used to maintain visibility.

The surgeon uses needle drivers to begin suturing around the large vessels in the lower retroperitoneum. A giant needle is employed for the procedure, and the focus is on the collecting system. The surgical team discusses the use of a micro four-oh suture.
The surgeon identifies the calyx and uses a larger needle than usual to place a stitch. Suction is applied to the area, revealing another calyx nearby.

The surgeon identifies a large vein and prepares to suture using a 3-0 PDS absorbable monofilament. The suture is used to re-approximate the medullary tissues over the sinus, aiming to close the dead space and address open venous sinuses.
The surgeon uses multiple sutures to create three layers, cinching them down sequentially. Needle drivers are used to manipulate the sutures, while suction is applied to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon observes bleeding from a segmental artery after removing both clamps. A hemo lock is applied to control the bleeding, with instructions to hook it securely in the corner.

The surgeon examines the surgical defect, using a suction device to clear the area for better visibility. A deeper exploration is performed, and a Hemlock clip is applied. The needle is removed, and the surgeon discusses the use of a bolster to manage bleeding, noting the effective ischemia achieved during the procedure.
